How to Configure and Host Resourcespace

Built entirely from scratch during my internship at mGanik. The company had 42TB of unorganised digital assets. I built the system to fix that.

## Stack

- **Storage:** Synology NAS
- **DAM frontend:** ResourceSpace (open source)
- **OS:** Linux (debian and ubuntu)
- **Networking:** Port forwarding, HTTPS, wildcard SSL certificates
- **Access:** SSH container management, NFS4 volume mounting

## What I learned

You can learn almost anything if you break it into small enough pieces and aren't precious about looking stupid in the process.

How to Host Services and Access them Externally

## What I learned

You can learn almost anything if you break it into small enough pieces and aren't precious about looking stupid in the process.

## Step 1: Make sure that you have port-forwarding enabled on your router and check with your ISP

I didn't know this but apparently many ISPs use this thing called CGNAT (Carrier-Grade Network Address Translation). In the internet everybody is addressed an IP Address, however,  in the entire world there's a limited amount of addresses for an ever-growing population of people using the internet. Therefore, ISPs need to distribute one public address with multiple people. While the up-side is that "YAYY everybody can get the internet" the down-side is that you can't use port-forwarding which is very important if you wanna host your own servers.

Academics aren't as important as people think for getting a job
Understanding how to get a job is completely different from being academically smart.

Just a disclaimer before you read this article, this isn’t meant to bash academics at all, but more just a reality check that being academic-smart is its own thing, and getting jobs is also its own thing.

For me, when I was applying to jobs, I was really scared to even start because I didn’t have a strong academic background. My grades were meh, and the university I came from didn’t really have a strong brand name either.

But along the journey of applying, speaking to recruiters, and slowly understanding how the job-finding process actually works, I realised that just like being good at exams or doing well in a course, there is also a certain set of conditions, rules, and things that employers look for when deciding whether to give you a job.

And I think that’s really my main point.

Being good at academics is one thing, but being employable is a completely different thing, and those two, in my opinion, do not always correlate as much as people think they do.

Maybe unless you’re trying to get into a super technical-heavy role, where you genuinely need that background knowledge and expertise, then yes, 100%, your degree, what you learned, and how well you did in it will probably matter a lot more.

But even then, I still believe you can be the most academically smart person and still not be able to land a role.

Because getting a job is not just about being intelligent in a classroom setting. It’s also about knowing how to position yourself, how to communicate your value, how to show relevant experience, how to make an employer believe that you actually understand the role and can do it.

And to be totally real, a lot of these things matter most when you’re trying to get into the very top companies. Even then, though, it’s not the end of the world if you don’t have perfect grades.

I was still able to get past a lot of first stages with a high 2:1, mainly because I made sure the experience I did have was relevant enough and made sense for the roles I was applying to.

So I think that was one of the biggest things I learned.

Not having the best grades does not automatically disqualify you. And not being the most academic person does not mean you cannot be employable.

They are just two different games, and a lot of people confuse one for the other.

Building an internal DAM system with no coding experience
How vibe-coding, a lot of Googling, and genuine stubbornness got me from zero to a working self-hosted Digital Asset Management system.

I thought that taking on this project might have been a bad idea. My boss, the founder, put me on a project to help finalize marketing assets more easily within mGanik. As a largely e-commerce-driven company, these digital marketing assets are what drive the business. They influence customer purchases through a strong focus on impressions while optimizing return on ad spend (ROAS).

As part of this project to revamp how marketing assets were made, I was tasked with dealing with a key problem.

The company had over 42TB of raw digital assets consolidated within an internal server. To create certain videos, or re-use old assets, the marketing team had to spend hours finding the exact file. The file-naming conventions were not only inconsistent, but also prone to human error. In practice, those conventions were often only understood by the people who originally shot the video assets, which made it a huge hassle when that exact person was unavailable or had already left the company years earlier.

So what was the solution? Simple: a Digital Asset Management (DAM) system.

Easy enough, right?

All I had to do was gather the team, follow the project management process already in place within the company, and execute.

I wish it had been that easy.

Following up on approvals just to move forward with the idea took ages. Technology team members were also scattered across different projects. I figured that if nothing could move forward at the beginning, I had better take matters into my own hands.

So I learned everything I could through AI, Google searches, documentation, and how-to guides.

I learned how to host a server, obtain secure HTTPS and wildcard certificates, coordinate with the ISP to port-forward access so the system could be reached online, use SSH to manage a container, manage permissions through Linux, set up the SQL back end and logins, understand how volumes within a container connect, and connect a volume to an external server through NFS4.

It was honestly the most intellectually demanding and abstract piece of work I had taken on, but that is also why I am probably proudest of it.

Especially when I showed the completed, working project to my peers and my boss—the gratitude and amazement on their faces is something I will never forget.

How to get a Grad Job in the UK as an International Student from a non-target Uni
My thoughts on how to actually succeed in the job market.

I am going to be honest, when I first started looking for a job, my pride would not let me apply to anything less than the best brand-name companies, the highest paying roles, and the hardest, most competitive positions to get into.

Sooner or later, I realized that not only was this ineffective, but I genuinely DIDN’T know what I wanted to do in life, who I was, or what skills I actually had.

To successfully land a job, you need to essentially convince the employer that you are genuinely interested in the industry and the you can bring value to the role that you’re trying to get into.

It’s really that simple. THAT IS IT.

However, when you try to apply for roles that you have no EVIDENCE of interest in, no projects for, and no actual knowledge about, it will show. It shows in your interviews, in your CV, and even in the type of personality you come across as.

It’s easy to WANT to get into finance. It’s easy to WANT to get into the top consulting firms.

However, showing that you are a capable person that can work in Finance, and that you have the ABILITY to actually make it in those industries, is a completely different thing.

Truthfully, the way to successfully make it past interviews is by understanding who you are and what you can actually bring to the table, and not half-assing it or lying your way through it.

When I first wanted to get into finance, I tried to learn as much as possible about macro trends, the right personality for finance, and what it meant to be a numbers guy.

But looking back, I think I was more obsessed with the idea of finance than actually understanding whether it fit me. I liked the prestige, the image, and what it represented. But that is very different from having real interest, real proof, and real ability.

That was probably the biggest shift for me. I stopped asking myself, “what is the most impressive job I can get?” and started asking, “what am I actually good at, what do I actually enjoy, and what can I back up with evidence?”

Because at the end of the day, employers can tell. They can tell when you are forcing it. They can tell when you are saying what you think they want to hear. And they can also tell when you genuinely understand the role, the industry, and yourself.

That is what helped me the most. Not trying to become this perfect candidate for every role, but becoming more honest about what I was actually interested in and building from there.

The biggest mistake I made at the start was thinking that wanting something badly was enough. It isn’t. Wanting to get into a competitive industry means nothing on its own. A lot of people want it and there's a huge chance that they're much more capable than you, so don't try to be the best, just try to be yourself.

What matters is whether you can show proof. Proof that you’ve taken the time to learn. Proof that you’ve explored the space. Proof that your interest is not just talk. And proof that you actually have something to offer.

As an international student from a non-target university, you already have enough stacked against you. So you cannot afford to be vague, performative, or unrealistic. You need to know your story, know your strengths, and be honest about where you fit.

That, more than anything else, is what helped me get a graduate job in the UK.

That, more than anything else, is what helped me get a graduate job in the UK.

How I led a Team to a National Final with Zero Leadership Experience

I never intended to be a leader. In fact, I had zero experience and even less of an idea of what the role actually required. My motivation was simple: I knew companies valued leadership, and I wanted to see if I could survive outside my comfort zone. I figured since I had no "leadership identity" to protect, I had nothing to lose by failing.

What followed was a journey from a dull first meeting to competing against the top university graduate business teams in the UK. This is the full recount of that journey.

The Starting Line: Turning Strangers into a Team
Our university entered six different teams into the Universities Business Challenge (UBC). To advance, we first had to compete in a simulation round against six other teams; only the top two would move on to the next stage.

Our first meeting was the opposite of a movie montage. It was awkward, uninspiring, and quiet. We were six strangers from completely different backgrounds—the UK, Europe, China, and Indonesia. While other teams seemed to be connecting instantly and building chemistry, we were still finding our footing. Ironically, out of the six teams from our university, only ours and one other made it to the next stage.

We had one crucial thing in common: we were there to win. I quickly realized that as a leader, I didn't need to have all the answers; I just needed to facilitate the environment where the answers could be found. I took on the heavy lifting—reading the dense documentation and setting the agenda—so the team could focus on flushing out the details. This allowed me to synthesize their insights into a cohesive strategy.

The Mechanics of the Challenge
The UBC wasn't a standard case study; it was a complex business simulation. We were essentially running a company in a digital ecosystem. Every week, we had to manage financial optimization (calculating variable and fixed costs), macro analysis (adjusting pricing based on global news), capital structure (debt vs. equity), and marketing ROI.

We ran intensive Excel calculations, tested our strategy in the early rounds, and held weekly "post-mortems" to analyze what went wrong. By delegating specific areas to team members based on their individual strengths, our strategy slowly became crystal clear.

The Semi-Finals: Pressure at Nottingham Trent
After surviving the preliminary rounds, we headed to Nottingham Trent University alongside the other qualifying team from our school. For me, there were two key takeaways from the semi-finals.

First, the environment changed. We were no longer working in the comfort of our own rooms with plenty of time; we were acting under intense, real-life competitive pressure. We had to act quickly, but also with energy and passion. Secondly, I noticed that the other team from our university was academically brilliant and conventionally "perfect"—they got along famously and seemed like the ideal candidates. To be honest, it made me feel a bit insecure. I questioned if we had the right to be there or if we were just lucky.

However, the tasks diversified and pushed us further. We weren't just crunching numbers; we had to be creative. One moment we were running simulations, and the next, we were pitching a creative advertisement for a product we’d just invented. This stage tested our ability to collaborate on the fly and challenged our fundamental perspectives on how a business should communicate.

The Grand Finale in Southampton
Against the odds, we made it to the national finals in Southampton. By the time we arrived, my team and I were exhausted. We had outlasted over 100 teams across the UK to get there. We pushed on with everything we had left, but the fatigue was real. Unfortunately, we didn’t secure a top-three placement.

While we didn't take home a trophy, I wouldn’t trade the experience for anything. Reaching the finals felt like a monumental accomplishment. I learned that leadership isn't about being the smartest person in the room—it's about having the courage to set the stage for others to succeed, even when you're learning the rules as you go.
